weather: Today in Edmonds: Partly sunny with intervals of clouds and sunshine. High 68-71°F, low 49-51°F. Light north-northwest winds 5-10 mph. Minimal rain chance (0-10%). Evening turns partly cloudy to clear. Pleasant spring day—light jacket for morning, comfortable afternoon.

local_news.findings: The Edmonds City Council approved key contracts and dropped potential tree litigation during its April 7 business meeting. The council unanimously approved a $415,116 contract with Jacobs Engineering for gasification system commissioning at the wastewater treatment plant, along with personnel changes including reclassifying the HR assistant to HR Analyst and restructuring wastewater treatment plant staffing. The council also voted 5-1 to voluntarily dismiss the city's counterpetition in the Redelfs tree litigation matter, where a 33-inch-diameter landmark tree was removed without a permit, resulting in a reduced fine from $102,245 to $20,000. Additionally, the council declared April 9 as Local News Day, with Mayor Mike Rosen signing a proclamation recognizing the value local news organizat [TRUNCATED]

The South County Fire Board of Commissioners is working on redistricting plans to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority's five commissioner districts. Two alternative plans have emerged: Plan 1 would consolidate almost all of Edmonds into a single district, while Plan 2 would split the city approximately at Pine Street to preserve existing commissioners within their districts. The process is guided by RCW 29A.76.010 requiring periodic redistricting as population demographics change, with public input being solicited before final adoption.

The Washington State Court of Appeals reopened a case involving Edmonds' tree regulations after previously dismissing it as moot. The case involves Edmonds resident Nathan Rimmer, who sued the city in 2023 alleging unconstitutional tree replacement requirements when building a home. The court initially dismissed the case after Rimmer sold the property, but his attorney filed a motion for reconsideration, arguing the city should still pay damages despite the property sale.

Edmonds approved a $415,000 contract with Jacobs Engineering to assist with operating the gasification system at its wastewater treatment plant. The technology, which converts human waste into dried solids and destroys PFAS 'forever chemicals,' has faced operational challenges since initial attempts in April 2023. After three years of development and $31 million in construction costs, the city hopes to begin consistent operations this month, with Jacobs Engineering providing support through 2027 before the city takes over unassisted operations in 2028.

The City of Edmonds is seeking public input on the Highway 99 Revitalization Project - Stage 3, which has reached 60% design completion. Residents are invited to complete an Urban Design Survey to provide feedback on the next phase of improvements along Highway 99.

The Edmonds City Council will consider a $383,456 amendment to a consultant contract for the second phase of a flow reduction study in the Perrinville drainage basin during its April 28 business meeting. This follows ongoing efforts to address environmental and infrastructure challenges in the area.

career.notes: Most postings are district-wide pools anticipating 2026-2027 vacancies rather than single, site-specific openings. Salary ranges are rarely listed explicitly; figures shown are district averages or summer-school daily rates when available. All positions require (or expect) a valid Washington State teaching certificate with the appropriate endorsement. Applicants should monitor district career pages and set job alerts, as new postings appear frequently through spring and early summer.

global_news.findings: Oil prices surged to a new wartime high above $126 per barrel Thursday as President Trump considers extending the naval blockade of Iranian ports. The Strait of Hormuz remains effectively closed, with shipping traffic reduced from 3,000 vessels monthly to just 154 in March. Iranian Oil Minister Mohsen Paknejad urged citizens to cut consumption, calling conservation "a religious duty" as the US mulls prolonged sanctions.

President Trump stated at a White House state dinner for King Charles that Britain's monarch "agrees with me even more than I do" that Iran should never have nuclear weapons. This marks the second day of Charles' four-day US state visit, coming amid tense US-UK relations over Trump's criticism of British support in the Iran war.

Ukraine accused Israel of receiving shipments of grain "stolen by Russia" from occupied Ukrainian territory, with President Zelensky warning that another vessel carrying such grain had arrived at Haifa port. Israel's Foreign Minister Gideon Saar rejected what he called "Twitter diplomacy" and demanded evidence through proper diplomatic channels.

The US and Iran clashed at the UN over Tehran's selection as a vice president for the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty conference. US Assistant Secretary Christopher Yeaw called it an "affront" to the NPT's credibility, while Iran's envoy Reza Najafi accused Washington of hypocrisy given its own nuclear arsenal. Iran proposed delaying nuclear talks until the war ends.

King Charles III addressed the US Congress, defending "democratic values" and highlighting the importance of continued US support for Ukraine against Russia, while avoiding direct mention of the Iran war. This came during his historic four-day state visit, the first by a British monarch since 2011.

intellectual_journals.findings: A sweeping investigation by ProPublica and FRONTLINE has documented how more than 300 U.S. citizens—protesters, legal observers, and even bystanders—were swept up in immigration-enforcement dragnets over the past ten months. Federal agents routinely labeled dissenters “domestic terrorists,” but more than a third of the cases collapsed once video evidence contradicted officers’ sworn statements. The pattern suggests a deliberate attempt to chill protest: even when charges are dismissed, the arrests themselves deter future demonstrations. One Marine veteran, Alejandro Orellana, was charged with  [TRUNCATED]

In a companion piece, ProPublica reconstructs a previously unreported December 2020 meeting at the Justice Department where Attorney General William Barr summoned cyber-security and FBI officials to answer a single question: could the 2020 presidential vote have been hacked? The specialists concluded that the Antrim County, Michigan “vote-switch” claim was human error, not fraud. Barr, recognizing that relaying this truth to President Trump would likely cost him his job, reportedly told aides he was about to “kamikaze” into the White House. The episode foreshadowed the broader erosion of elect [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova’s long essay on Frances “Fanny” Wright recovers the forgotten 19th-century Scottish-American radical who inspired the young Walt Whitman. Raised on Enlightenment philosophy and granted rare access to the University of Glasgow’s library, Wright wrote “A Few Days in Athens” at eighteen—a philosophical novel championing Epicurean ethics and social justice. Emigrating to the United States, she founded the experimental commune Nashoba to test interracial equality and free love, scandalizing contemporaries but shaping American progressive thought. Whitman would later recall her as “a br [TRUNCATED]

In a separate essay, Popova revisits Jorge Luis Borges’s 1946 “A New Refutation of Time,” arguing that Borges offers a poetic antidote to our modern obsession with productivity and chronology. Drawing on Berkeley and Hume, Borges treats time not as an external flow but as a cognitive illusion: each instant contains all eternity, and personal identity is a “bundle of perceptions” rather than a continuous self. Popova links Borges’s insight to contemporary neuroscience and mindfulness practice, suggesting that recognizing time as a construct can loosen its grip on our anxieties. The essay frames [TRUNCATED]

The LRB’s April 23 issue (Vol. 48 No. 7) carries a cluster of essays reflecting on the erosion of democratic norms on both sides of the Atlantic. While the table of contents is brief, it signals forthcoming pieces on the politics of memory in post-Brexit Britain and the legal philosophy of emergency powers. The issue’s framing suggests a sustained LRB preoccupation with how liberal democracies justify—and survive—illiberal measures. Print subscribers can expect the issue to arrive by May 7, with digital access already live for the journal’s app subscribers.

triadic_ontology.findings: Three 2025-2026 works advance triadic-relational metaphysics: Migliorini’s monograph fuses trinitarian theology with analytic ontology to ground divine relationality beyond substance monism; the new Karl Alber volume “Trinitarian Ontologies” inaugurates a series bridging analytic and continental approaches, tracing triadic thought from late antiquity to modern anthropology and politics; and Isabelle’s “Triadic Relational Ontology” argues that dyads are insufficient for emergence—an irreducible, actively maintained relational field is required for intelligibility after primordial separation.

ai_systems.findings: Three papers this week push autonomous research from lab demos to hardened production systems. Google Research’s PaperOrchestra (arXiv:2603.28986) introduces a 12-agent pipeline that writes, peer-reviews, and submits full research papers end-to-end; in trials it produced 37 conference-ready drafts in 48 hours with 94 % acceptance at ICLR 2026 workshops. A second paper from MIT CSAIL (arXiv:2602.08990) shows how to chain GPT-4.5-level reasoning models so that each agent critiques the prior agent’s work, cutting hallucinations by 61 % on multi-hop science questions. Finally, a Stanford/Anth collaboration (arXiv:2601.19170) ships an open-source “Agent-as-a-Judge” framework that replaces human reviewers with a meta-agent whose scores correlate 0.89 with human peer review, enabling 24-hour journal turnaround. Prompt-engineering front: MagicSchool’s April update adds “reasoning tokens” that expose intermediate chain-of-thought to teachers, while Diffit’s new “explain-your-steps” toggle lets  [TRUNCATED]

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
The actress Ellen Burstyn has what she calls an “inner library” of poetry that she has memorized in the course of her long life. “I’m ninety-three now, so I probably have less in my head than I used to when it comes to poems,” she said the other day. “But some have been there for years. Decades.” Burstyn was in her Upper West Side apartment, which was festooned with books, plants, crystals, orbs, and statues of Shiva and Buddha and Guanyin (“the goddess of compassion,” she noted). Meditative music piped in through the sound system. “I was getting a massage, so it’s left over,” she explained.
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come rea [TRUNCATED]

literary_pick.title: The Brief Wondrous Life of Oscar Wao
literary_pick.author: Junot Díaz
literary_pick.summary: A Pulitzer Prize-winning novel that follows the multi-generational story of the de León family, centering on Oscar, an overweight Dominican-American sci-fi nerd cursed by the "fukú" that haunts his family. The book blends Dominican history, comics, magic realism, and footnotes to explore themes of identity, love, and the immigrant experience in America. Critics have hailed it as the best novel of the 21st century so far, praising its innovative style and profound exploration of the American experience.

# Hands-on with Bee, Amazon's latest AI wearable

In early tests with a review unit of [Bee](https://www.bee.computer/), we found the device itself was easy to use. It's just a press of a button to turn recording on or off.
